original_abstract: Numerous peptide hormones adopt $\alpha$-helical conformations upon receptor binding, and the development of sensitive detection methods for these molecules is critical for improving clinical disease management. While de novo protein design has successfully produced high-affinity binders for structured proteins, designing interfaces for short peptides with high helical propensity remains a formidable challenge. In this study, we present a synergistic approach utilizing parametric generation and deep learning to overcome this limitation. By extending the RFdiffusion framework to accommodate flexible targets and implementing a "partial diffusion" strategy—which refines structural models through iterative noising and denoising—we generated binders with picomolar affinity. These binders were produced either by optimizing initial templates or entirely de novo from random noise, achieving high potency without the need for experimental maturation. We demonstrate that these RFdiffusion-designed proteins facilitate the mass spectrometry-based enrichment of parathyroid hormone and glucagon and enable the development of modular bioluminescent biosensors. The capacity to design binders for conformationally plastic targets and to refine proteins via partial diffusion represents a significant advancement in computational design with broad therapeutic and diagnostic utility.
humanized_abstract: Several peptide hormones change to an α-helical conformation upon binding to the receptor and there is a great need to develop sensitive assays to diagnose clinical diseases. Although de novo design of proteins that act as high affinity binders for structured targets has been demonstrated, design of interfaces between short peptides that can exist with high α-helical content is an extremely challenging task. Here we show that peptide/protein interaction design, which combines the use of parametric generation and deep learning (based on the RFdiffusion framework and a new method that we name “partial diffusion” where iterative noising and denoising refines the structure of a protein to improve its target binding), results in proteins that give picomolar affinities to their target peptides. These proteins were either optimized from a starting point (e.g. a known antibody surface) or were designed de novo from total randomness. In addition to serving as targeting molecules for increasing the sensitivity of mass spectrometry assays to detect peptides and proteins of interest, they can also serve as modular bioluminescent biosensors, where we show the feasibility of using them to enhance the mass spectrometric detection of parathyroid hormone and glucagon. Overall, design of proteins that target flexible conformational states of peptides or proteins and the development of proteins that benefit from the refinement provided by the partial diffusion process, opens many therapeutic and diagnostic opportunities.
